speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...has written a book of her childhood recollections called Coming West from South Dakota,  and is working on a companion volume that will go from the 8th grade to her marriage.  Her writing is rich in personal and family anecdotes that show what pioneer life was like.  So is this conversation, which looks further at some of the whys and wherefores in Coming West from South Dakota.  At the end, Mrs. Fleener recalls some of what her father-in-law, Dave Fleener,  told her about his crossing the plains at the age of four in 1852.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I can't just remember any of the instances right now about what I should have put in, but...  Well, I remember at the time we had head lice.  We had a teacher, I know it was a teacher of some of the school kids that had head lice, and we got it too.  You know what head lice is, don't you?  Well, it's lice in your hair, huh?  Yeah. It was hard to get them out, you know.  My mother, she combed and combed with a fine-tooth comb and muttered all the time about it.  I never got it.  Of course, we had quite long hair then, and it was quite an ordeal to get it out.  And of course, they had nits, eggs, you know, that hatch out too.  I know we had turpentine on our head or kerosene or something.  I know it was terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that the details, they just keep coming to you?  Oh, yes.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Especially at night or in the evening or morning early.  I don't know why.  I think everybody should have the same opportunity to remember it, but they don't seem to.  They don't think they do anyway, but I think if they didn't have anything else on their mind, they probably could.  I tell them because I don't think about it.  I don't have anything particular to worry about, you know.  I can think of these things that I wouldn't ordinarily probably, but...  I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If I tried to write about my childhood even now, I think I couldn't come up with the kind of richness of detail that you have.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I'm surprised.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wondered if it had something to do with the times.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It might have been too, you know.  I think I have remembered more about my early childhood than I have later on.  When my...  Oh, you know.  The details during the...  My college time and things like that.  Of course, I remember a lot of things that happened then, but then probably not the many details.  What is it that made you feel that it was important to put your past, your recollections down in writing?  I want my grandchildren to know.  I want them to know how it was when I was their age, you know.  Or how it was in the old days.  That's my principal reason for doing it, you know.  We didn't have telephones, electricity, or refrigeration or anything like that, you know.  It's hard for me to believe that now myself.  I know that, you know, since I've never experienced it, would not know, you know.  We had the kerosene lamps we had to carry around with us, you know, upstairs to bed.  It's a wonder we didn't burn the house down or fall down with them sometimes.  It would be terrible if we broke a lamp chimney too and didn't have a light, you see.  There's so much hard work about cleaning lamps too, you know.  The trim had to be just right.  The chimneys had to be washed.  The lamps had to be filled with kerosene.  We would think that would be an awful drudge nowadays.  Just that little bit, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that it is hard.  It's hard.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I think it is too.  If you haven't lived through it.  Even if we had lived through it, looking back, I don't know how we ever got along.  But then we didn't know anything different, you know.  That's the reason we got along so well.  The farmers we have living was terrible too, you know.  The hard work they did.  Yeah.  Yeah.  Yeah.  Tell them about that in the next book.  About there.  About the farmers did, you know.  To till their land and break their horses.  And get out and milk the cows in the wintertime.  And go to town in the winter in a sled.  Do them anyway.  And getting there in the car, you know.  For years and years.  Come in.  Your next question.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I don't really have questions so much.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But do you think the children grew up more quickly in those days?  I don't really think they did.  I don't think they knew as much.  I know I didn't know as much.  When I was 20, they do now.  And they're 10 or 12, I don't believe.  The general thing is, of course, they knew a lot about lots of things.  Because we grew up in the country, you know.  And there's lots of things to know about the outdoors that people and children don't know now.  I think it's a shame, really, that they quit the country schools myself.  Because I enjoyed myself so much during those years.  Even though I walked a mile or so to school.  Always something interesting.  Of course, we didn't know anything about anything different.  Took our lunch and we always had fun at school, you know.  It was really enjoyable.  No dread of even going to school at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's funny because, you know,  you get the impression that nowadays schools are a lot more progressive.  There's a lot more that kids can do than they used to be able to.  And it used to be more strict.  But maybe that's not quite the way.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, there's two sides to it, of course.  Of course, you couldn't take band and a lot of things they take now in school.  But I think that the general outlook on life was better then than it is now.  I think children grew up with a greater understanding of what the principal things are in life.  It's worthwhile.  I mean, worthwhile things.  Seems to me like they do.  You see lots of country boys and girls that have made themselves names, you know.  Just from being country kids, you might say.  The big inventors and all were country boys.  And the boys and the girls.  So I've read.  I'm not really much for education myself.  College education, I think it frustrates people.  You know, they don't know which way to go after they take that.  It wakes them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say principles, the basic principles, what do you have in mind?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, nature is one thing.  And...  I don't know.  I just can't hardly explain it.  There's just so much you get out of going back and forth through the open air that you don't get when you rush in on a bus or something.  I don't know why it is, but it seems like we do get a lot more from that.  From something, I can't say what it is, but...  Something really enjoyable anyway.  Birds and everything that you see along the way is really interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe it has something to do with taking things for granted.  Well, probably.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Of course, I was brought up real straight, but I think that most people were in that age.  They were more...  Well...  I wouldn't say religious exactly, but...  More...  Devoted to their parents and...  More honest and trustworthy and all that, I think.  General rule, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I get the impression that you're a little more...

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I get the impression that at the same time that you were more sheltered, perhaps, than people are now.  I think so, too. I know we were.  We didn't get to know a lot of things we should have known by reading and getting out.  We had a very limited amount of reading.  And we didn't get out and mix with people very much, and my folks didn't say so-and-so bad because he does so-and-so and things like that.  You know, they never...  Brought to mind that...  Why and wherefore of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most people were like your parents?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well...  To some extent, I think so.  All the school kids that we went to school with, I know they were...  Honesty was one of the main things.  Well, they wouldn't hire a teacher that wasn't perfectly...  A good model anyway, you know.  They couldn't smoke or drink or...  Anything like that.  Do anything that was out of the way.  And of course the kids all used the teachers and the models to go by.  I think that's one thing that's kind of corrupted the country now, too.  The kids can't have respect for the teachers they had like we used to.  I think we had more respect for our parents, all of us, than they do now, too.  Can't think of anybody in the neighborhood that didn't anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there rules that you were tempted to break?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Oh yes, I wanted to dance awful bad and they wouldn't let me. They were ethnicists, you know, and they didn't care if they were  playing or dancing or anything like that. Well, I vowed when I was 18 I would dance  anyway, but I was too old to... I didn't have much of a chance for one thing. And then I  felt as though I was too old to learn at that time. I didn't want to make a spectacle of  myself. It's because we didn't have cars, you know, they were hard to get out and make  with people anyway after I moved over into Whitman County. We were kind of off from any  of the young people at that time, so...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It does seem though that you have a lot of experience with the music, you know,  that you got more experience with work and...

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yes. Yes, I started working out when I was quite young and worked around the neighborhood  all over, a week or two at a time, you know, here and there. That was after that book,  I mean, this next book here. And of course I... I suppose I learned, got a lot of experience  that way along the work line. I think I... I would not...  I was a door person. I helped my dad and I helped neighbors. I was a big person to sew.  I liked to sew and so I come in handy that way. I didn't pay very much and I couldn't  get a head very much, you know, that way. It was about enough to buy me clothes and  that's all. Five dollars a week for seven days sometimes, so it didn't add up very much,  you know. Of course, what you bought didn't cost very much either, but then...  Yeah.  I bought the piano and hoped to pay for it that way. I never got quite paid for it.  And...  $800, but I thought I'd go to college and...  and learn to teach, but I didn't want to teach. I didn't want to teach any time. I wasn't  meant to be a teacher, I don't think at all. But I thought that I would teach to get some  money so that I could take me to the library to get a job.  I didn't want to do education. I knew good and well I couldn't be an expert in music  anyway. I'd just be amusing myself. So I was used to doing LFC. I was kind of up a creek  whichever way I turned, so I got married instead.  I thought my college days would be quite an uplift. I thought I would be able to live  in a good place, you know. I thought I'd do pretty well because I was good in languages  in high school, but it wasn't the same in college. They would... took German and he  never could... never could play as a teacher. So I decided I wouldn't go in for languages.  There wasn't anything else in college that I cared for. Only music. I couldn't take his  course and I didn't have the money to pay for it, so... I had to work for my board too,  which was another thing I couldn't... enjoy myself very much. It kept me tied down, you  know. Couldn't go out very much in the evenings or anything. I could go roller skating a time  or two, but I never learned entirely about roller skating either. So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they have agriculture at the college then?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, girls couldn't. I wanted to take animal husbandry more than anything, but they wouldn't  allow girls to take that at all. I don't think they... I don't know. I don't think they had  agriculture. They called it...  They called it...  They called it the

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The other thing was, was it... was the first time that you worked out when you worked for  Aunt Hattie?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well... I worked out for... When I was about ten, ten years old for a week I think. And  I was so homesick I couldn't stand it, hard I'd get the ball in my own time. Didn't I  say something about that?  That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's right. I read that earlier and then I read the last part just recently and I've forgotten.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I did most of my working out after I was 15 or so. The neighbors here and there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that true of most families? Did the girls in most families work out?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, I don't think they did. But my sisters could stay at home, you know, and it wasn't any use of both of us being home.  And so I was ambitious to earn some money, I guess.  People seemed to want me to sew and I liked to sew, so there we went.  I always have been kind of a fiend to earn money.  I always got myself a jackpot though, usually.  When I was married I used to raise sheep and chickens and pigs and everything like that, you know, thinking I'd get a little bit for myself.  I got ready to sell it with something else in the house around that needed worse, so I never got my brothers.  I was all the time doing those things, letting the house, neglect the house, you know, and I'd be back in the house, I'd be too tired to keep the house up.  So...  I was bad that way too.  Couldn't win.  But then after all it was a good life. I have no regrets about it at all now.  Sounds like you just were really good and liked animals.  Why did I love animals more than anything else?  Sneak out and milk the cows. I told the kids that's the only time I had vacations and I got out and I milked cows.  I didn't have time to think then, you know.  I milked other things other times.  I didn't have time to think.  I was just getting the milk around, you know, and things to do and think about.  So...  My husband didn't like to milk cows, so we got along pretty well that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think boys were allowed to do a lot more than girls were?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I don't know. I can't say.  I don't know.  They made themselves pretty well, I think. I don't know.  Of course, they got out and skated more than the girls did and got out and played in the snow more than we did, I think.  Like that.  But I don't think they...  Wanted a place to go, you know. Hardly had to go foot or horseback or...  Like that.  And that's too much trouble in the wintertime.  Summertime, they were working during the day and big boys, that was.  I'd say.  And so they were too tired, I guess, to go then.  I don't know about the kids in town, you know.  I didn't...  We didn't see much of those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they given more responsibilities?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Boys?  I don't think so.  The girls, they had their responsibilities in the house, you know.  When we had to wash and iron and everything like that, it took a lot of time.  All our clothes, all of them were starched, you know, and ruffled and...  All that took a lot of time.  I ruffled some pleats and...  Ironed on the stove, you know, heated them on the hot stove.  Yeah.  Summertime was really a...  Bad thing to have to do with iron in the summertime with a hot stove to heat the irons on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the boys liked school as well as the girls did?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, they seemed to.  I don't know.  Nobody seemed to dislike school anyway.  In the country school, they all seemed to take it with a come.  We had our spelling matches after school and our ciphering matches and everybody just thought that was great, you know.  Some Friday afternoon, rather.  So...  Did you have any friends?  Oh, I see.  No.  The kid was his teacher.  Why the teacher would put...  When the boy got on and put him in a double seat, you see.  The way they punish him and put him with a girl.  I should've put that in the first book too.  Now, they wouldn't care would they?  Now they just seem to be mean on purpose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it wouldn't work anymore.  It would be good.  But you've done enough with it.  You do say something like that in the book when you talk about the game, that breaking  out of jail or whatever, that the girls liked it better than the boys did.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah.  My mother used to have three long-haired girls to comb their hair from on or braid their  hair, you know, and comb it.  I don't know whether it's mentioned that in the book or not, but two of my sisters  had real long hair.  Especially the...  My younger sister, Viola, she had hair that could clear down to her knees, finally.  Maybe by that time she could have combed her own hair, but then she had thin, heavy hair.  My older sister didn't take my hair, it never grew very long, but at least they had to be  all combed before school, you know, before we went to school.  And we had our work to do before school, of course.  The boys had...  I don't think they went out and helped milk like some boys did, because my dad always  thought that he could do that.  He was very particular.  But then there was wood to carry in and water to get, water to pump maybe for the stalks  and things like that.  I only had one brother that was of any size anyway when I was growing up.  He was younger than me.  By the way, his birthday was Sunday, or Monday.  And...  Yeah.  Yeah.  And the other two brothers were the youngest in the family, so they were just little babies,  you might say, at the time.  Lived over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So about how long did it take after you got up in the morning before you could go to school?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I don't know how long it took.  I have no idea.  If we didn't get through, we'd be late for school, I know.  If the folks wanted us to help them with the washing or anything like that, it wasn't  necessary.  We'd stay out of school.  Nobody cared if we stayed out of school.  Authorities didn't care, you know.  People didn't care anything.  It's like now they raise the dickens, you know, if you're out of school to help anybody,  help at home, anyway.  How many months of the year did you go to school?  I don't think there were nine months.  I think it was about six months.  Eight, some months, or less than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you got done with the school day and you got home, did you have mostly just free  time?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I don't remember about that.  It seemed like it was the late time we got home and about supper time, anyway.  We didn't get out until four o'clock and then we had to walk home.  It was pretty near dark in the wintertime.  I suppose the boys had to, the brother had to get in the wood.  Yeah.  The girls helped get supper and wash the dishes afterwards and like that.  After supper, I guess all free time.  I know we used to sit around and play dominoes or popcorn or something like that, you know.  Then we went to bed quite early, too, because we had to get up quite early in the morning.  I don't have any idea how early it was, though, but I think my dad got up before light in  the wintertime and went out and did the chores, milked the cows and fed the horses.  Dad buttered the make, buttered the churn, you know, and all those jobs that we don't  think of anymore about anybody ever doing in their life, you know.  There's always something coming up to be done.  I used to take the butter to town, too, when I was growing up.  My job was to drive one horse buggy to town, take the butter in the store and get the groceries.  Really fun.  By the way, I nearly always came home with a headache because of the hot sun.  I started out at six o'clock in the morning.  I remember that.  So it would be cool if the butter wouldn't melt on the way, you know.  About noon I got back and it was hot, about eight miles to town.  I really liked to drive the old horse.  I ate the buggy because it wasn't painted, didn't have any top, it looked so scraggly  and I wanted to be ashamed of it.  But it went anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you just turn around and come right back after you got the groceries?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, usually I did.  Sometimes I did.  I just turned and bought some cloth or a dress.  One time I ordered them twice.  I went to the ice cream parlor and had some ice cream.  That was a real treat, I know.  I don't know how it came I did because I wasn't supposed to spend the extra money for anything.  I must have been, I must have got permission to do it.  It was great.  I didn't have to get permission, too.  I was sixteen years old.  That's the way they were.  They didn't do anything without permission.  Do you think that this was about how most of the families were?  Do you think that your family was stricter than most?  Well, I think it was stricter than most, but then I think they were all pretty well strict.  I think the youngsters obeyed their parents and respected them and all that.  Yeah.  I think that some of them were allowed to dance, you know, until they didn't go anyplace.  They didn't go to town as far as that goes, you know, to crowd around.  Nobody allowed them into public dances.  They had public dances in Moscow then.  Everybody was at them, you know, and that was supposed to be kind of a bad place to go because of so much riff-raff, I guess, drinking.  Moscow had a lot of Sloans in town at that time.  Everybody don't know what the...  what the...  what the...  Obviously, they wanted to keep them out, but then they just didn't want to mix with that kind of a crowd, I guess.  Everybody seemed pretty well satisfied to stay home, you know.  Yeah.  I can't remember anybody being dissatisfied about not...  about wanting to go or to do anything different.  I wasn't thinking that it was any... that it was any less good than the way they do it now, but it just seems so different.  Yeah, it was so different.  I know that.  I suppose these people are used to living in this era and would hate to go back to that, be a humdrum life, I imagine, but...  we didn't know anything different, so it didn't matter to us.  That's... that's what makes a difference, I think, because we didn't know any different.  Just like the farmers, how they...  would hate to go back to the same old drudgery kind of work they did in those days.  They couldn't stand it, for one thing, and they're not used to it.  But...  they didn't know any different then, so...  what could they do?  In the days when they had binders, you know, and it was...  bounder... grain, that was a great step forward.  But then it took man to shock it up.  You know, it was a hard back-breaking job to...  around putting up all those shocks of sheaves of grain that were heavy...  to put up, you know, it took a long time to do it.  And then they had to get them over with the thrashing machine and thrashing them, you know,  and then they had the heavy sacks to pick up to put all of them out of the way.  Now they do it all with a combine, you know, one man does it and puts it in the bulk tank and...  later, save all that trouble.  But my...  I... there's so many people that have back trouble these days, I wonder if it's just because they don't use their back.  Well, they don't think people had back trouble like they do now.  Used to bend down, you know, and pick up things, heavy things.  Can't use my hands to do any sewing with or anything, and I just thought to myself, uh...  put down some of these things that I've...  remember when I was a kid, just to show my grandkids what...  what it used to be like to live where I am, and so I just kinda started doing all of them.  Just kept coming, coming, coming all the time.  Couldn't stop it, you know.  I'd say.  I'm sorry to disappoint, I can't use my hands, that's what I was gonna do when I got old.  I was gonna do some fancy work I never had much time to do when I was younger.  Big family, youngster.  And before that I had to work, work with my board and everything when I was going to school.  I didn't get much chance to do these things I wanted to do, but...  I'm glad I can pick this out.  I don't know how long my hand's gonna last, but...  This one's getting kinda crippled up now, but then...  I won't worry about it as long as I do, so...  I'm gonna put it to the...  But this one is...  I'm not gonna get any more and do anything with it as far as typing is concerned.  I'm gonna keep it to myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe you could explain a little to me about...  How well off different families were.  Did they measure, did families measure how well off they were like families do these days?  I don't mean in the same terms, because there wasn't as much money, but did they think a lot about how well they were doing?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I don't know about that.  I guess maybe they did.  I think that...  I know one of our neighbors close to us was a...  I don't know what he thought, but then I thought that he is...  Had things pretty good.  And...  Put up a new house.  He had a nice bunch of cows.  Beautiful, slick cows that they had.  And...  But the Munsons that...  I spoke of there, and their father was in the state legislature, and I presume they had more money than we did, but then...  We were kind of jealous of them because their kids had more shoes than we did, I know.  As far as that, well, we always did friends, and we never...  We acted always on the same footing.  The youngsters did anyway, you know, and I think that the...  The parents in general did too, all over the country.  Most of the farms were small.  They couldn't say, well, we have so and so hundred acres of the ground.  We farm, and we have this, and we have that, you know.  They didn't do that, I'm sure.  I know what I think about.  Of course, I say my folks were very careful about not running anybody down anywhere, in any way.  So we didn't have any idea that we were going to be in trouble.  But everybody was just as...  Just as good as our parents were in every way possible.  My dad was always thoughtful and...  Decent sort of man.  We always thought all of them were, I guess, a general thing they were, I guess.  But I found out later they haven't been, you know.  Because the more I see of the world, or I have seen of the world, I know there's other kind of people.  But...  My dad only had 80 acres, and that wasn't enough for him to farm.  Because he had to milk so many cows in order to make anywhere near a living.  That's the reason he wanted a bigger place so he could farm more with machinery and horses.  I think most of the places around there were coastal mountains, and they had a lot of evergreen trees on them.  They didn't have very much farming to do.  And the Robinson Lake area, maybe you know where that is.  So the farther they got out that way, the less cultivated ground.  We weren't in debt that I know of.  Where I didn't know, I think he...  My dad knew he tried to pay cash for everything at that time.  Of course, everybody did as far as they could.  I think they didn't go in debt like they do nowadays.  When we moved over to Whitman County, why he had to...  We had to go in debt then to get 160 acres.  And...  He had a $7,000 mortgage on the place.  And he worked like everything we saved, like everything.  He was afraid he was going to lose it if he didn't make the payment every year, you know.  $1,000 to pay every year with interest.  He made it all right, and made lots of money besides because he rented the place next to him.  But he worked so hard, he really...  It was too much for him until...  Bad for his health.  He died when he was 70, 72, I think.  So...  I think part of it was overworked.  But you know, in the old days, they would foreclose on the land.  Those people would sell land to banks, would sell land to a farmer at a big price and brag it up.  And then the farmer couldn't make his payment, so they'd take it away from him.  They'd foreclose on it.  That was a regular thing there for a while.  I guess that continued.  It didn't until Roosevelt's time.  He stopped that foreclosing.  I'm not sure, but he well may have.  Some...  It hadn't been too recent that that can't happen.  Of course, nowadays, people don't want land.  These people don't want land so bad as they used to.  Anyway...  I mean, it wasn't that way.  Maybe they do now, but then...  I know that...  We have a mortgage with a mortgage company.  We've helped our boys out getting farming, starting to farm.  And they say they don't want the land at all.  They want us to make the payments all the time.  They want to make it so we can make the payments easy.  Because they don't have use for the land anyway.  I think that's the general run of things.  They want to be bothered with land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there is a stronger feeling,  a very strong feeling of getting ahead?  Or was the feeling more of just getting by?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I think they just wanted to make enough money  to have something left over to be sure they wouldn't be caught with nothing.  Of course, sickness and things like that took money in those days.  Nowadays, they don't have the safeguards that they need.  They didn't used to have the safeguards they have now,  in some respects.  Medicare and...  I guess it's up on our younger people now, too,  to finance their sicknesses, operations and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the feeling of cooperation was very strong?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Oh, yes, it was definitely strong.  It is now, my goodness, yes.  People are closer together.  Of course, they couldn't get away very far,  so they depend on each other for help and entertainment and all that, you know.  Here, we don't hardly know who our neighbors are sometimes, you know,  because we go off to the lake or someplace on Sunday or evenings or something.  We go to a show or something.  We don't have anything to do with that.  And, we've got something to do,  but it seems like that they hardly know our neighbors anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it mainly a few families that your family was close to

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: and spent most of their time with?  Well, there was one particular, the Monson family,  where we got our children who were the same age as ours.  The same families.  Mr. Monson was the first one.  He wasn't home very much at the time,  but when he was home, he was pretty good about taking us to things.  He took us to middle areas.  He even took us to Juliaetta one time on the Fourth of July.  But, you see, the people had small places.  They lived pretty close together, much closer than they do now in the country.  160 acres apiece or something like that by...  Your neighbor is pretty close.  Where they were close, in walking distance, that way.  Instead of singing school and literaries and reading circles,  all the reading circles didn't last very long.  And...  That petered out pretty quick.  Many people weren't good readers.  They weren't all interested in the same reading.  You know that...  See...  The Masalis Mariner was what they were trying to read.  That's a pretty dry book, you know.  I remember...  I don't think many people were interested in it.  They never got through.  Of course, the children made noise, you know, and interrupted.  They interrupted, more or less.  They thought it would be quite educational for us.  Did they read it out loud?  Oh, yes.  It took turns reading, you know.  Some of the people weren't very good readers.  It's hard to keep everybody's attention.  It was in the wintertime, and it was hard for people to get together, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the literaries?  What went on in that?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The literaries.  What went on in the literaries?  Old people spoke pieces and had dialogues.  And one school would come to another one and give their program, or the program it was, you know.  And...  Sing songs and...  entertain you, you know.  And then maybe that...  Your school would go over to their school or go to some other school.  Another district, you know.  The districts weren't too far apart.  District schools.  I suppose that...  Three miles apart, probably.  The Pine Grove and the...  And the Peasant Valley were...  Sometimes the children from...  Pine Grove would come down to our school if our school was only a seven-month school.  They were up farther in the mountains, and I think that...  It was hard for them to always finance...  some of the schools.  Why the people weren't...  I don't know how they did finance them really in those days.  But I know in the poorer districts, the schools, they were shorter termed.  The adults would attend these literaries too?  Oh yeah, oh yes.  Quite often they took part in them too.  That was what they did.  The people of the district all turned out and wanted to give a program.  Not only just to kids, but the older people too, the adults.  They'd...  Perform and...  Usually they'd give some comical...  Readings or something, you know.  I guess I told you about the dogs and the...  One...  One school put on it for us.  Had a big hopper and threw the dogs in there and they ground out sausages on the other side.  I guess I spoke about that in the book, didn't I?  Those kind of things they did, you know.  Something unusual like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were the kind of people that your family didn't want you to associate with?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I can't remember anybody around there they didn't want to associate with.  I don't mean names or anything, but I just had the feeling that...  That...  There were certain...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Elements that weren't thought of as undesirable.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well...  I can't think of anybody, anything in particular.  I didn't want to associate with the drunkards, of course.  The drinking people or the...  Fast girls or...  Like that.  Of course, there wasn't much danger anyway because we never got out at night.  Town, there wasn't anybody...  And then they would come out in the country.  So...  I can't think of anyone that would be out of bounds to us.  We knew anything about it anyway.  I don't know.  I don't know.  I don't know.  I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The difference that you mentioned between your father and your mother.  Your father was plain as an old shoe and your mother had slight aristocratic interests.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah.  Yes, that was true all through her life.  She wanted us all to marry.  Well...  She...  She wanted to get me married off for some reason or other.  I don't know what.  It's because she saw I didn't have any prospects of anything else, I guess.  She set my wedding date and everything.  I was 22 years old in that grade.  But she tried to stop my sister Viola from marrying the man she did.  She insisted on it.  And him not seeing him for a year, she bet she'd forget him.  And she wanted to marry some college fellow that Viola had slightly gone with a little.  No.  It's because he had an education.  And Leo wouldn't get an education at all.  He wouldn't...  He didn't even have a high school to get.  But he's very...  He's a genius in making things.  He's an American mechanic.  He made the first trash machine they had right from scratch.  And...  So I...  I'm inclined to be more my line with Dad.  I think there's...  Another thing besides college...  That doesn't make a person, I mean.  Being a college graduate.  Yeah.  Mom?  But I would have always built my own private college.  I think I did some...  I think I used myenge to not get a job. vic-  But I using it all for that practice.  Clean clothes.  What was graduate school?  Oh man, I didn't think...  I am...  And live your own good life.  But then you were about 18 years old.  AJ.  Never soaped an ants.  for some reason or another.  And it might've been that she got that trait inherited  from the other side of the family, you know,  the other side.  Of course, my dad, his folks were not,  well, his sister got to be quite an elocutionist too.  She did, she traveled around quite a bit,  giving readings and all.  But most of his family were just ordinary people, you know.  I guess they could have been something else  if they had a chance for an education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say she set your wedding date  when you were 22, does that mean before you were ready?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I don't know.  Sometimes I think maybe I'd never gotten married at all  if it hadn't been that she did that.  But then we didn't have her doing what she wanted us to do,  see, and setting the pace for us  because she thought the best that,  we felt that it was best for us to do that  ever since we were kids.  And so we just automatically,  and of course it wasn't very hard for me to swing into it.  And I was sort of shocked to think it'd be so soon.  And we were supposed to have a double wedding,  my older sister and I.  Something happened to them and they never did get married.  She married somebody else later, but.  My sister and I were never very close either.  She was, she stayed around the house a lot.  I kept out of her, out of her way a good deal of time.  I said in the book it was easier to do that  and it was to quarrel with her.  She was our dominating person, you know.  Kept us busy all the time if we were around the house,  kind of bossing us around.  I'd get out and mend sacks with my dad  and help him in the barn.  I read my mother's diary here.  My daughter got it typed up.  Her name was pretty dim.  She made it before she had any family, any children at all.  She was real good at bringing down her feelings,  but the way she expanded out a little bit more  on detail here and there,  almost all about her feelings and this and that kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is when she was in South Dakota?  Oh, yes, uh-huh.  Were her feelings when she was there optimistic mostly?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, she got real religious.  Really, yeah.  just for one thing. She praised the Lord for all the good things that happened to her and  all the wonderful man that she had. My dad was awful good to her, I know that. I think  it was terrible because she kind of, the last years of her life she went to California and  left him up here half sick. He wanted to find a place to live. He couldn't live in the house  alone very well. I know he liked to stay in his own house the last few years of his life and  had really a home to stay in if he was sick. He moved around from one family to another,  his kids. It wasn't the kind of way he wanted to live at all. He wanted to settle down and be  quiet. She wanted him to go to California. He did try it down there, but it wasn't a home to  him at all.  So he came back here. She liked to go every place down there and everybody went  any place she went to. I don't know whether it's going down in tape or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it is. That's okay. Was it harder on one or the other of them to leave South Dakota and  come out here in the first place?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, I don't think it was. My mother was glad to because she didn't like to be in the storms  and everything back there.  I think she was a little bit more of a, you know, a little bit more of a, you know, a  little bit more of a, you know, a little bit more of a, you know, a little bit more of a, you know,  I think my dad, well, it might have been harder on him because he was doing well and he'd gotten  on his feet and he was on a farm and he was, got work and she's way clearer than him. Makes  money, I guess, when it comes to equities or out of debt or something. I don't know, but to  understand that he was a little reluctant to come. I guess that he didn't, maybe he just wasn't  quite so  quite so daring, I guess I'd say, about coming. When he got here, he didn't see how he could  ever farm the hills, you know. It took him a long time to figure out that he could and  he wanted to get the level of space he could find and the land kept going up and going  up all the time. She kept picking him on and she got a place picked out over here about  Pullman that she wanted to go and see and some way or another they didn't get over there  to see it and it was sold. And she said in her book that if it had been that they'd gone  over there, they might have been Pullmanites by this time. I guess you didn't see her book,  did you? She wrote a book, too. In her 80s, I guess she was 80.  I did experience it when he came up here, you know. His mother, they crossed, he crossed  the plain in 52 with his family and the Ox team. Of course, he was only four years old,  but he could remember something about the trip. His mother died on the way and a brother  of mountain fever, cholera, you know, the epidemic of it on that year or those years,  on 52. And his mother died in Platte River and Long Platte River and his brother died  in South Idaho somewhere. Yeah, I've heard him say that if he, if he could go to South  Idaho, he bet he could find the very place where his brother was buried. It made such  an impression on him, of course. He couldn't, of course, everything would change, but it  was so plain to him that he realized it's a plain, you know. He thought he could go  back there and see it, find it.  I wrote that up, what I can remember them telling about it. Then he came up here in  Whitman County and, of course, it was Washington territory at that time. But he kind of had  quite a lot of experience here in the early days, you know, 1877, that was.  Yeah.  It was very sparsely settled at that time here.  What did he say that it was like then when he first came?  Well, it was an open country. There wasn't any neighbors close by at all. Of course,  some of the land had been taken up, some of the best land was taken up around Moscow,  but he, Moscow was just getting started then and Mr. Glelland, he wanted to get the town  started.  He wanted to get the town started, so he offered him a lot in town where the first  security bank is now if he would build a shack on it, you know. Any kind of a building he  could have a lot. He wasn't interested in town property, so he would turn it down. Then  he offered him a lot if he'd trade his horses later. He'd trade his horses for a lot. He  wouldn't give up his horses because he couldn't farm without them, so he didn't take that.  But then, first winter, he was here in 1877, it was an open winter and he didn't have much  of a, needed much of a house. He just had a kind of a tent and a tarp over the top and  board floor. I guess he brought some up with him from Walla Walla or Pomeroy where he'd  been herding sheep down there for a while for his dad. And then he had, he hobbled one  horse out and the horse stayed around close.  But,  But,  He did have another bachelor friend that just across the fence from him.  They used to run around together quite a bit.  Some people came across the road from him, and what across the road he is now.  The woman didn't want to stay because she knew that they never have any closer neighbors  and that.  She wanted to go to stay so they never unpacked anything and left.  Finally the family came there and they were real close.  Family was along real well together.  Then he married, he was 40 years old.  They said that the barn that they put up on this Collins place, it was right next to the  place that he had.  They had him going up the, putting the pins in the top.  The barn is all put together with pegs, not nails, but pegs.  They had him putting the roof on or the rafters up there because he was the only unmarried  man there.  He got married soon after that though.  He was an old man when my husband was born.  He was born in the 40s.  He was 40 years old when he married.  My husband grew up kind of an alone chick.  His mother died when he was two and a half years old.  My brother was growing up with his dad.  Dad, I don't think ever took very much pains about him.  He was used to living alone and he just kind of let the kid along the best he could.  My husband told me about different times when he pretty much put off the way dad treated  him.  Just remember, he was a little bit older.  He'd probably around 60 seconds or so being around  and got the horse out and took the shovel back.  He stopped and talked to these neighbors, you know,  visited with them.  The kid was cold and probably wet  from walking in that deep snow all the way down there  and tired and never forgot it.  And another time he was telling about his dad,  left him to milk the cow and went off  and he never milked it before.  Cow kicked him and called him out of milk.  And went off in a tricycle or something like that.  So he never did like to milk cows.  He claimed that was the reason.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seems it must have taken Mr. Gray courage  for Mr. Fleener to keep on after his mother  and brother died.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I know it was.  I guess he was pretty well brokenhearted when he,  when the boy died, he said that his father  made the right remarks.  That if he brought enough medicine along,  that he could have cured it.  They wouldn't have needed him and died.  Well, he gave it, he really started out  with enough medicine for him own self.  And then he might've shared with other people  that were sick before, you know,  and didn't have enough for them afterwards.  But the way they ended up, they were sitting out at Oxon.  They stopped someplace at Trading Post in South Idaho,  somewhere, I don't know, Fort Boise or Fort Laramie.  No, and then he didn't know, but someplace down there  where they exchanged.  Well, the immigrants could exchange the tired animals  for some good one, you know, better one.  Well, they exchanged for a young milk cow.  It stood up with one ox and the head left  and came the rest away.  The cow gave milk for the family and seeing the kids.  It was all of them under 12.  And there's one of them two years old.  The youngest one was two.  And the husband's father was four and there was Tom,  and Jane, and it's been five, I guess, left.  They were met at the Dells by his brother  that had come out here before.  My husband's father's brother had come out here  to Oregon before, they're headed for Oregon, see.  And I guess the train had gone on left him,  probably saying a word that they were coming.  And so he met him at the Dells.  That was a hard hump to go over there anyway, the Dells.  And I know he said that he never saw such fat,  big fat oxen as that in all their life.  They hadn't seen anything but scrawny thin ones, I suppose.  You know what I'm saying?  Been months on the road.  They started in May.  I don't know what time they got to Oregon,  sometime before winter, but that time be quite a bit.  A lot of courage, I bet you.  But they said this cow was given to the youngest daughter,  Nancy, her name was Nancy.  That was the youngest one.  She got married and it was given to her.  The cow was old by that time, you see.  And they soon butchered her.  She never ate any of the meat.  She was such a pet, I suppose, you know.  Brought them all the way through the rest of the way  from South Idaho to the Dells, Oregon.  However, she had a lot of ambitions.  These two guys would see her in a field battle,  or they say in the ...
